content.problemStatement:
Santosh has a farm at Byteland. He has a very big family to look after.  His life takes a sudden turn and he runs into a financial crisis. After giving all the money he has in his hand, he decides to sell his plots. The speciality of his land is that it is rectangular in nature. Santosh comes to know that he will get more money if he sells square shaped plots. So keeping this in mind, he decides to **divide his land into minimum possible number of square plots**, such that each plot has the **same area**, and  the **plots divide the land perfectly**. He does this in order to get the maximum profit out of this.

So your task is to **find the minimum number of square plots with the same area, that can be formed out of the rectangular land, such that they divide it perfectly.**


Input Format

The first line of the input contains TTT, the number of test cases. Then TTT lines follow.
The first and only line of each test case contains two space-separated integers, NNN and MMM, the length and the breadth of the land, respectively.


Output Format
For each test case, print the minimum number of square plots with equal area, such that they divide the farm land perfectly, in a new line.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains T, the number of test cases. Then T lines follow, each containing two space-separated integers, N and M, the length and the breadth of the land, respectively.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the minimum number of square plots with equal area, such that they divide the farm land perfectly, in a new line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, the GCD of 10 and 15 is 5. So, the minimum number of square plots with equal area is (10 * 15) / (5 * 5) = 6. For the second test case, the GCD of 4 and 6 is 2. So, the minimum number of square plots with equal area is (4 * 6) / (2 * 2) = 6.

solution.approach: To find the minimum number of square plots, we first need to find the greatest common divisor (GCD) of the length and the breadth of the land. The GCD will be the side length of the square plots. Then, we can calculate the minimum number of square plots by dividing the total area of the land by the area of each square plot.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * log(min(N, M))) — because we are using the Euclidean algorithm to find the GCD, which has a time complexity of O(log(min(N, M)))
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — because we are only using a constant amount of space to store the input and the output
